verses.text: And after this it came to pass, that David smote the Philistines, and subdued them: and David took Metheg-ammah out of the hand of the Philistines. Guidance: A summary chapter follows the theology chapter. Chapter 7 has established the LORD's promise; chapter 8 shows the geographic expansion that the promise empowered. The campaigns reported here probably span several years and are summarized for thematic effect. The first conquest is the Philistines — David's lifelong enemies from his Goliath days. He 'subdued them' (kana — broke their political power). 'Metheg-ammah' is the puzzle. The Hebrew literally means 'bridle of the mother-city'; the parallel passage in 1 Chr 18:1 reads 'Gath and her towns,' suggesting Metheg-ammah is a phrase for the chief Philistine capital and its dependencies — David took the bridle, the controlling authority, of the Philistine confederacy. The pentapolis that had dominated Israel for over a century is now a Davidic vassal. The chapter is going to walk through the conquests one nation at a time. The Philistines come first because their subjugation is the most psychologically significant — they had been the existential threat throughout Saul's reign and David's wilderness years; David has now broken them.

And he smote Moab, and measured them with a line, casting them down to the ground; even with two lines measured he to put to death, and with one full line to keep alive. And so the Moabites became David's servants, and brought gifts. Guidance: Moab next. The Moab campaign is harder to read morally. David has the Moabite captives lie on the ground and measures them with a cord: two cord-lengths are killed; one cord-length is spared. Two-thirds of the male captives executed; one-third kept alive as tribute-paying subjects. The harshness is striking. Moab is David's ancestral kin — his great-grandmother Ruth was a Moabite (Ruth 4:17), and he had once placed his parents under Moabite protection during the wilderness years (1 Sam 22:3-4). Why the severity? Possibly Moab had broken faith with the family of David in some later betrayal; some commentators speculate that Moab killed David's parents during the wilderness years (an extra-biblical tradition). The text does not explain. What it does record is the result: 'the Moabites became David's servants, and brought gifts.' Moab becomes a tribute state. The narrator does not editorialize on the percentages; the Old Testament records its kings' wars without softening them. The chapter is showing the full historical record, including the parts that are difficult to read in retrospect. Faithful historiography does not airbrush.

David smote also Hadadezer, the son of Rehob, king of Zobah, as he went to recover his border at the river Euphrates. David took from him a thousand chariots, and seven hundred horsemen, and twenty thousand footmen: and David houghed all the chariot horses, but reserved of them for an hundred chariots. Guidance: The northern campaign. Hadadezer king of Zobah is one of the major Aramean kings of the period, ruling territory between Damascus and the upper Euphrates. He was extending his border northeast 'to recover his border at the river Euphrates' — i.e., expanding his territory; the chapter does not say whether this provoked David's preemptive strike or whether David was extending Israelite influence at the same time. The capture is enormous: 1,000 chariots, 7,000 horsemen (1 Chr 18:4 reads 7,000; KJV's '700' here may be a textual variant), and 20,000 foot soldiers — a major standing army. David 'houghed' (hamstrung) all the chariot horses except enough for 100 chariots. Hamstringing — cutting the tendons of the rear legs — was the standard Israelite practice when capturing horses they had no use for: the Torah forbade Israelite kings from 'multiplying horses' (Deut 17:16), and chariotry was not the Israelite military tradition. David keeps only a small chariot force — perhaps a ceremonial guard — and disables the rest. The chapter shows him being financially astute about the capture (he gets the brass, the gold, the spoil) and theologically restrained about the equipment (no large chariot force). The kingdom is expanding, but not into a horse-and-chariot empire. The Davidic monarchy will remain primarily an infantry power, in conformity to Deuteronomic norms.

And when the Syrians of Damascus came to succour Hadadezer king of Zobah, David slew of the Syrians two and twenty thousand men. Then David put garrisons in Syria of Damascus: and the Syrians became servants to David, and brought gifts. And the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went. Guidance: The Damascene Arameans intervene to help Hadadezer. David crushes them too — 22,000 killed. The Damascene Arameans become a third tribute-state, with Israelite garrisons stationed in Damascus itself. The chapter is showing the systematic expansion: Philistines (west), Moab (east), Zobah (far north), Damascus (north). David's reign reaches further than any king's before it. The narrator's repeated theological refrain: 'The LORD preserved David whithersoever he went.' This is the same phrase from 1 Sam 18:14 ('the LORD was with him'), now expanded to a foreign-policy claim. David's military success is not the product of his strategy; it is the product of God's preservation. The clause will recur in v14 to bracket the campaign narrative. The whole chapter is between two statements of 'the LORD preserved David' — that is the chapter's theological frame for everything in between.

And when the Syrians of Damascus came to succour Hadadezer king of Zobah, David slew of the Syrians two and twenty thousand men. Then David put garrisons in Syria of Damascus: and the Syrians became servants to David, and brought gifts. And the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went. Guidance: The Damascene Arameans intervene to help Hadadezer. David crushes them too — 22,000 killed. The Damascene Arameans become a third tribute-state, with Israelite garrisons stationed in Damascus itself. The chapter is showing the systematic expansion: Philistines (west), Moab (east), Zobah (far north), Damascus (north). David's reign reaches further than any king's before it. The narrator's repeated theological refrain: 'The LORD preserved David whithersoever he went.' This is the same phrase from 1 Sam 18:14 ('the LORD was with him'), now expanded to a foreign-policy claim. David's military success is not the product of his strategy; it is the product of God's preservation. The clause will recur in v14 to bracket the campaign narrative. The whole chapter is between two statements of 'the LORD preserved David' — that is the chapter's theological frame for everything in between.

And David took the shields of gold that were on the servants of Hadadezer, and brought them to Jerusalem. And from Betah, and from Berothai, cities of Hadadezer, king David took exceeding much brass. Guidance: The spoil. The gold shields — ornamental armor carried by Hadadezer's royal bodyguard — go to Jerusalem. Brass (or bronze; the same Hebrew word covers both copper alloys) is taken from Betah and Berothai, two Aramean cities. 1 Chronicles 18:8 tells us this is the brass Solomon will use to make the great basin (the 'molten sea') and other temple vessels. The detail is striking: David's military campaigns are accumulating the materials for the temple Solomon will eventually build. The temple project that David proposed in chapter 7 and the LORD reassigned to Solomon is being funded, materially, by David's wars. The LORD's 'no' to David personally was not 'no' to David's contribution; it was a redirection. The materials gathered by the father will become the building constructed by the son. The chapter is showing us how God's larger projects work across generations. Faithful work in one lifetime sets up the harvest of the next.

When Toi king of Hamath heard that David had smitten all the host of Hadadezer, Then Toi sent Joram his son unto king David, to salute him, and to bless him, because he had fought against Hadadezer, and smitten him: for Hadadezer had wars with Toi. And Joram brought with him vessels of silver, and vessels of gold, and vessels of brass. Guidance: Toi, the king of Hamath (a major Aramean city-state further north on the Orontes River), opens diplomatic relations with David voluntarily. Toi had been at war with Hadadezer; David's defeat of Hadadezer removed Toi's most dangerous enemy. Toi sends his own son Joram (probably Hadoram per 1 Chr 18:10; the names are interchangeable in early Hebrew) with congratulatory tribute — silver, gold, brass vessels. The chapter is showing us a foreign king who realizes Israel is now a regional power and pays the diplomatic price of recognition before David has even campaigned against him. There is a category of military victory that is so total it produces voluntary alliances from previously unconquered neighbors. Toi's gift is not the result of David's conquest of Toi; it is the result of David's defeat of Toi's enemy. Sometimes the LORD's victories extend their benefit to allies the LORD's anointed had not even been seeking. The Davidic kingdom is becoming the regional hegemon by reputation, not just by direct conquest.

Which also king David did dedicate unto the LORD, with the silver and gold that he had dedicated of all nations which he subdued; Of Syria, and of Moab, and of the children of Ammon, and of the Philistines, and of Amalek, and of the spoil of Hadadezer, son of Rehob, king of Zobah. Guidance: The treasure consecration. 'David did dedicate unto the LORD' — the verb hiqdish, set apart for sacred use — all the precious metals from his conquests. The roster of nations is sweeping: Syria (Aram), Moab, Ammon, Philistia, Amalek (the Amalekite victories from his Ziklag days), Zobah. The whole eastern, western, northern, and southern frontiers have contributed metal to the LORD's treasury. Note the theology: David does not keep the gold and silver as personal royal wealth. He dedicates it. The temple Solomon will eventually build is being funded from a treasury that already belongs to the LORD before construction begins. There is a category of king whose first instinct with new wealth is consecration, not accumulation. David sets the standard. The contrast with later kings who used royal wealth for personal pleasure is built into the chapter's careful inventory. The kingdom is being built; the LORD is the actual beneficiary; the future temple is being underwritten years in advance.

And David gat him a name when he returned from smiting of the Syrians in the valley of salt, being eighteen thousand men. And he put garrisons in Edom; throughout all Edom put he garrisons, and all they of Edom became David's servants. And the LORD preserved David whithersoever he went. Guidance: The southern campaign. 'The valley of salt' is the Arabah south of the Dead Sea, the natural battleground between Israel and Edom. The casualties are 18,000 (1 Chr 18:12 and the title of Psalm 60 attribute the same number to Edomites, suggesting the manuscript here may have suffered a transcription error and the enemy is Edom, not Syria; the campaign in any case is against the southern federation). David 'gat him a name' (asah shem — made a name for himself) — the same phrase the LORD used in 7:9 ('I have made thee a great name'). The Davidic reputation is now international. Garrisons go up throughout Edom; the trans-Arabah trade routes to the Red Sea and the copper mines of the Arabah are now under Israelite control. The chapter ends the campaign section with the second occurrence of the theological refrain: 'The LORD preserved David whithersoever he went.' The frame is closed. Everything between v6 and v14 has been bracketed by the same clause. The kingdom from Egypt to the Euphrates, the prophetic promise the LORD made to Abraham (Gen 15:18), is essentially complete under David. The patriarchal land-grant is, geographically, achieved. The chapter is recording the largest Israelite kingdom in biblical history; nothing larger will ever exist again.

And David reigned over all Israel; and David executed judgment and justice unto all his people. Guidance: One verse on the domestic side of the reign. 'David reigned over all Israel' — the unified-kingdom reality. Then the key summary clause about his internal administration: 'David executed judgment and justice unto all his people.' The Hebrew is mishpat utzedaqah — the central royal-virtue pair throughout the Old Testament: mishpat (legal due process) and tzedaqah (right relationships, equity). The king-as-judge image, going back to the ancient Near-Eastern royal ideal, is realized in David. He does both — both adjudicates disputes with proper procedure and acts so as to produce right-relations across society. 'Unto all his people' — not just to the elite, not just to his tribe, not just to the wealthy. All. The chapter is showing us that David's external conquests have a parallel reality at home: just governance for all Israelites. The verse is the highest compliment 2 Samuel pays to David's kingship in its inventory of his characteristics. Later kings will be measured against this standard. When the Israelite prophets in the eighth and seventh centuries denounce the corruption of the monarchy, the implicit reference is this verse — the original Davidic ideal that the later dynasty failed to maintain.

And Joab the son of Zeruiah was over the host; and Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud was recorder; And Zadok the son of Ahitub, and Ahimelech the son of Abiathar, were the priests; and Seraiah was the scribe; And Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was over both the Cherethites and the Pelethites; and David's sons were chief rulers. Guidance: The royal cabinet roster. Six positions and one note on the king's sons. (1) Joab son of Zeruiah — commander of the standing army; the same Joab from chapters 2-3, still in office despite Abner's murder. David could not remove him; he remains the most powerful general in Israel. (2) Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud — recorder (mazkir, the 'remembrancer'; the senior bureaucratic officer who kept records and probably managed royal correspondence). (3) Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar — joint high priests. The narrator has been careful: Zadok (the line that will become dominant under Solomon and remain so until the exile) and Abiathar's son (the line from Eli, salvaged from the Nob massacre, that will be deposed in 1 Kings 2 when Solomon exiles Abiathar). Both serve under David in parallel — the political compromise between the two priestly lines is preserved. (4) Seraiah — scribe (sopher, secretary, probably in charge of state correspondence and possibly literacy and education). (5) Benaiah son of Jehoiada — commander of the Cherethites and Pelethites, David's foreign mercenary bodyguard (probably ex-Philistine and Cretan soldiers). Benaiah will eventually replace Joab as army commander under Solomon. (6) David's sons — chief rulers (kohanim, the same word for 'priests'; here it probably means 'chief ministers' or court counselors, since David's sons could not have been Israelite priests — they were not Levites). The cabinet is balanced, complete, professional, and includes both Israelite and foreign elements. The chapter ends with the picture of an established government. The throne is not just personally David's anymore; it has the staffing infrastructure of a real state. The Davidic kingdom has become an administered nation. The throne is ready for what 2 Samuel will show us next — the moral test of chapter 11 and the kindness-test of chapter 9. Tomorrow, chapter 9, we see how David exercises his power toward the surviving Saulide — Mephibosheth. The cabinet is set; the kingdom is established; the king must now show what kind of king he will be in office.
